attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

{
  "name": "sw-em24",
  "version": "v0.1.0",
  "description": "COR24 emulator (cor24-run) with built-in assembler. Serves as the vendored cross-assembler for sw-as24 during bootstrap, until the self-hosted assembler can assemble its own source.",
  "repo": "https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs.git",
  "repo_path_local": "../cor24-rs",
  "commit": "40033d90e80dcef1a420bd3db7c8fd22fb9f181f",
  "tag": "TBD",
  "build_cmd": "cargo build --release --bin cor24-run",
  "build_cwd": "rust-to-cor24",
  "binary_src": "rust-to-cor24/target/release/cor24-run",
  "headers_src": null,
  "docs_src": "docs",
  "platforms": {
    "linux-x86_64": {
      "sha256": "TBD",
      "size": 0
    }
  },
  "recorded_at": "2026-04-18",
  "recorded_by": "relaunch-saga",
  "notes": "cor24-rs is the stabilized upstream (GitHub sw-embed/cor24-rs). It is the parent of sw-cor24-emulator (the sw-embed fork intended for future changes); while the fork has no tracked divergence, we vendor from cor24-rs. The cor24-run binary lives in the `wasm2cor24` package under rust-to-cor24/, which is outside the workspace members, so `cargo build --release --bin cor24-run` must be run from that subdir (see build_cwd). Binary path is relative to repo root (binary_src). Bump commit + platforms.sha256 when re-vendoring."
}

file.content:
# sw-cor24-x-assembler

COR24 assembler library — parses COR24 assembly language and produces machine code.

Part of the [COR24 ecosystem](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project).

## Dependencies

- [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator) — ISA definitions and CPU types

## Build

```bash
./scripts/build.sh
```

## Usage

```rust
use cor24_assembler::Assembler;

let mut asm = Assembler::new();
let result = asm.assemble("lc r0,42\nhalt:\nbra halt");
assert!(result.errors.is_empty());
println!("{} bytes of machine code", result.bytes.len());
```

content.content:
1	//! COR24 Assembler
2	//!
3	//! Parses COR24 assembly language and produces machine code.
4	//! Uses encoding tables extracted from the hardware decode ROM.
5	//!
6	//! COR24 is a C-Oriented RISC 24-bit architecture with:
7	//! - 3 general-purpose 24-bit registers (r0, r1, r2)
8	//! - 5 special registers: fp=r3, sp=r4, z=r5, iv=r6, ir=r7
9	//! - Single condition flag (C)
10	//! - Variable-length instructions (1, 2, or 4 bytes)
11	//! - 16MB address space (24-bit)
12	//! - Little-endian byte ordering
13	
14	pub mod assembler;
15	
16	// Re-export main types for convenience
17	pub use assembler::{AssembledLine, Assembler, AssemblyResult};
18	
19	// Re-export emulator types that assembler consumers typically need
20	pub use cor24_emulator;
21

1	# sw-cor24-assembler -- self-hosting COR24 assembler.
2	#
3	# Build glue over the vendored cor24-run (pinned by vendor/active.env
4	# + vendor/sw-em24/*/version.json). Recipe logic lives in scripts/;
5	# justfile recipes are one-line delegates.
6	#
7	# `just --list` renders the single comment line immediately above
8	# each recipe as its description, so keep those one-liners.
9	
10	# Load vendor/active.env so $SW_EM24_VERSION is in recipe env.
11	set dotenv-load := true
12	
13	# Show the recipe list.
14	default:
15	    @just --list
16	
17	# Materialize vendor/sw-em24/<v>/bin/cor24-run from the sibling repo.
18	vendor-fetch:
19	    ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh
20	
21	# Verify every vendored artifact is present; exit non-zero if missing.
22	vendor-check:
23	    ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh --check
24	
25	# Assemble src/sw-as24.s into build/sw-as24.bin via vendored cor24-run.
26	build:
27	    ./scripts/build.sh
28	
29	# Run the smoke test: sw-as24 vs cor24-run byte-identical on nop.s.
30	test:
31	    ./scripts/test.sh
32	
33	# Assemble + execute a user .s file through vendored cor24-run.
34	run file:
35	    vendor/sw-em24/$SW_EM24_VERSION/bin/cor24-run --run {{file}}
36	
37	# Remove the build/ directory. No-op when already absent.
38	clean:
39	    rm -rf build/
40

file.content:
# sw-cor24-assembler -- self-hosting COR24 assembler.
#
# Build glue over the vendored cor24-run (pinned by vendor/active.env
# + vendor/sw-em24/*/version.json). Recipe logic lives in scripts/;
# justfile recipes are one-line delegates.
#
# `just --list` renders the single comment line immediately above
# each recipe as its description, so keep those one-liners.

# Load vendor/active.env so $SW_EM24_VERSION is in recipe env.
set dotenv-load := true

# Show the recipe list.
default:
    @just --list

# Materialize vendor/sw-em24/<v>/bin/cor24-run from the sibling repo.
vendor-fetch:
    ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh

# Verify every vendored artifact is present; exit non-zero if missing.
vendor-check:
    ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh --check

# Assemble src/sw-as24.s into build/sw-as24.bin via vendored cor24-run.
build:
    ./scripts/build.sh

# Run the smoke test: sw-as24 vs cor24-run byte-identical on nop.s.
test:
    ./scripts/test.sh

# Assemble + execute a user .s file through vendored cor24-run.
run file:
    vendor/sw-em24/$SW_EM24_VERSION/bin/cor24-run --run {{file}}

# Remove the build/ directory. No-op when already absent.
clean:
    rm -rf build/

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	#
3	# vendor-fetch.sh -- materialize vendored toolchain artifacts from
4	# the manifests committed under vendor/<tool>/<version>/version.json.
5	#
6	# This project vendors exactly one tool: sw-em24 (the COR24 emulator,
7	# upstream sw-cor24-emulator, binary cor24-run). cor24-run is used
8	# during bootstrap as both the cross-assembler (to build src/sw-as24.s)
9	# and the emulator (to run the resulting binary). Once sw-as24
10	# self-hosts (plan.md saga 13), the vendored tool is retained only
11	# for regression testing.
12	#
13	# Usage:
14	#   ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh           -- fetch all tools
15	#   ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh --check   -- verify artifacts exist
16	#   ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh --help
17	#
18	# Dependencies: bash, jq, cargo (in the sibling repo, for --release).
19	
20	set -euo pipefail
21	
22	SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
23	REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
24	VENDOR_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/vendor"
25	ACTIVE_ENV="$VENDOR_DIR/active.env"
26	
27	cd "$REPO_ROOT"
28	
29	# --- Argument parsing -------------------------------------------------------
30	
31	MODE="fetch"
32	
33	while [ $# -gt 0 ]; do
34	    case "$1" in
35	        --check)
36	            MODE="check"
37	            shift
38	            ;;
39	        -h|--help)
40	            sed -n '2,18p' "$0" | sed 's/^# \?//'
41	            exit 0
42	            ;;
43	        *)
44	            echo "error: unknown argument: $1" >&2
45	            exit 2
46	            ;;
47	    esac
48	done
49	
50	# --- Load active.env --------------------------------------------------------
51	
52	if [ ! -f "$ACTIVE_ENV" ]; then
53	    echo "error: $ACTIVE_ENV not found" >&2
54	    echo "hint: ensure step 003-vendor-manifest has landed in this branch" >&2
55	    exit 4
56	fi
57	
58	# shellcheck source=/dev/null
59	. "$ACTIVE_ENV"
60	
61	# --- Helpers ----------------------------------------------------------------
62	
63	manifest_get() {
64	    # $1=manifest path, $2=jq filter expression
65	    jq -r "$2" "$1"
66	}
67	
68	resolve_local_repo() {
69	    # $1=manifest path
70	    # repo_path_local is relative to the repo root.
71	    local local_path abs
72	    local_path="$(manifest_get "$1" .repo_path_local)"
73	    if [ "$local_path" != "null" ] && [ "$local_path" != "TBD" ]; then
74	        abs="$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && cd "$local_path" 2>/dev/null && pwd)" || true
75	        if [ -n "${abs:-}" ] && [ -d "$abs/.git" ]; then
76	            echo "$abs"
77	            return 0
78	        fi
79	    fi
80	    echo ""
81	}
82	
83	# --- Fetch sw-em24 (cor24-run) ---------------------------------------------
84	
85	fetch_em24() {
86	    local version="${SW_EM24_VERSION:?SW_EM24_VERSION unset in vendor/active.env}"
87	    local dest="$VENDOR_DIR/sw-em24/$version"
88	    local manifest="$dest/version.json"
89	    local expected_path upstream
90	
91	    echo "--- sw-em24 $version ---"
92	
93	    if [ ! -f "$manifest" ]; then
94	        echo "error: manifest not found: $manifest" >&2
95	        return 1
96	    fi
97	
98	    expected_path="$(manifest_get "$manifest" .repo_path_local)"
99	
100	    if [ "$MODE" = "check" ]; then
101	        if [ -f "$dest/bin/cor24-run" ]; then
102	            echo "  cor24-run: ok"
103	            return 0
104	        else
105	            echo "  cor24-run: MISSING"
106	            echo "  hint: run ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh to fetch"
107	            return 1
108	        fi
109	    fi
110	
111	    # Three resolution strategies, in order of preference:
112	    #   1. $SW_EM24_BIN -- explicit path set in active.env or the
113	    #      shell environment. Highest priority: a dev can always
114	    #      override by pointing at a known-good binary.
115	    #   2. Upstream sibling + cargo target/release (the canonical
116	    #      build-from-source flow this manifest describes).
117	    #   3. System-installed cor24-run on PATH (last-resort fallback
118	    #      on dev systems that don't follow the sibling-repos
119	    #      layout).
120	    # Pick the first that yields an existing file; copy it into
121	    # the vendored bin/.
122	
123	    local cor24_src=""
124	
125	    if [ -n "${SW_EM24_BIN:-}" ]; then
126	        if [ -f "$SW_EM24_BIN" ]; then
127	            cor24_src="$SW_EM24_BIN"
128	            echo "  source: SW_EM24_BIN override ($cor24_src)"
129	        else
130	            echo "error: SW_EM24_BIN is set but $SW_EM24_BIN is not a file" >&2
131	            return 1
132	        fi
133	    fi
134	
135	    if [ -z "$cor24_src" ]; then
136	        upstream="$(resolve_local_repo "$manifest")"
137	        if [ -n "$upstream" ]; then

165	        echo "  tried (in order):" >&2

167	        echo "    2. $expected_path/target/release/cor24-run" >&2
168	        echo "                                  -- sibling not populated or not built" >&2

170	        echo "  to fix, pick one:" >&2
171	        echo "    - export SW_EM24_BIN=/path/to/cor24-run  (or set it in vendor/active.env)" >&2
172	        echo "    - git clone https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator.git $REPO_ROOT/$expected_path" >&2
173	        echo "      && (cd $REPO_ROOT/$expected_path && cargo build --release)" >&2
174	        return 1

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# vendor-fetch.sh -- materialize vendored toolchain artifacts from
# the manifests committed under vendor/<tool>/<version>/version.json.
#
# This project vendors exactly one tool: sw-em24 (the COR24 emulator,
# upstream sw-cor24-emulator, binary cor24-run). cor24-run is used
# during bootstrap as both the cross-assembler (to build src/sw-as24.s)
# and the emulator (to run the resulting binary). Once sw-as24
# self-hosts (plan.md saga 13), the vendored tool is retained only
# for regression testing.
#
# Usage:
#   ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh           -- fetch all tools
#   ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh --check   -- verify artifacts exist
#   ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh --help
#
# Dependencies: bash, jq, cargo (in the sibling repo, for --release).

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
VENDOR_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/vendor"
ACTIVE_ENV="$VENDOR_DIR/active.env"

cd "$REPO_ROOT"

# --- Argument parsing -------------------------------------------------------

MODE="fetch"

while [ $# -gt 0 ]; do
    case "$1" in
        --check)
            MODE="check"
            shift
            ;;
        -h|--help)
            sed -n '2,18p' "$0" | sed 's/^# \?//'
            exit 0
            ;;
        *)
            echo "error: unknown argument: $1" >&2
            exit 2
            ;;
    esac
done

# --- Load active.env --------------------------------------------------------

if [ ! -f "$ACTIVE_ENV" ]; then
    echo "error: $ACTIVE_ENV not found" >&2
    echo "hint: ensure step 003-vendor-manifest has landed in this branch" >&2
    exit 4
fi

# shellcheck source=/dev/null
. "$ACTIVE_ENV"

# --- Helpers ----------------------------------------------------------------

manifest_get() {
    # $1=manifest path, $2=jq filter expression
    jq -r "$2" "$1"
}

resolve_local_repo() {
    # $1=manifest path
    # repo_path_local is relative to the repo root.
    local local_path abs
    local_path="$(manifest_get "$1" .repo_path_local)"
    if [ "$local_path" != "null" ] && [ "$local_path" != "TBD" ]; then
        abs="$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && cd "$local_path" 2>/dev/null && pwd)" || true
        if [ -n "${abs:-}" ] && [ -d "$abs/.git" ]; then
            echo "$abs"
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    echo ""
}

# --- Fetch sw-em24 (cor24-run) ---------------------------------------------

fetch_em24() {
    local version="${SW_EM24_VERSION:?SW_EM24_VERSION unset in vendor/active.env}"
    local dest="$VENDOR_DIR/sw-em24/$version"
    local manifest="$dest/version.json"
    local expected_path upstream

    echo "--- sw-em24 $version ---"

    if [ ! -f "$manifest" ]; then
        echo "error: manifest not found: $manifest" >&2
        return 1
    fi

    expected_path="$(manifest_get "$manifest" .repo_path_local)"

    if [ "$MODE" = "check" ]; then
        if [ -f "$dest/bin/cor24-run" ]; then
            echo "  cor24-run: ok"
            return 0
        else
            echo "  cor24-run: MISSING"
            echo "  hint: run ./scripts/vendor-fetch.sh to fetch"
            return 1
        fi
    fi

    # Three resolution strategies, in order of preference:
    #   1. $SW_EM24_BIN -- explicit path set in active.env or the
    #      shell environment. Highest priority: a dev can always
    #      override by pointing at a known-good binary.
    #   2. Upstream sibling + cargo target/release (the canonical
    #      build-from-source flow this manifest describes).
    #   3. System-installed cor24-run on PATH (last-resort fallback
    #      on dev systems that don't follow the sibling-repos
    #      layout).
    # Pick the first that yields an existing file; copy it into
    # the vendored bin/.

    local cor24_src=""

    if [ -n "${SW_EM24_BIN:-}" ]; then
        if [ -f "$SW_EM24_BIN" ]; then
            cor24_src="$SW_EM24_BIN"
            echo "  source: SW_EM24_BIN override ($cor24_src)"
        else
            echo "error: SW_EM24_BIN is set but $SW_EM24_BIN is not a file" >&2
            return 1
        fi
    fi

    if [ -z "$cor24_src" ]; then
        upstream="$(resolve_local_repo "$manifest")"
        if [ -n "$upstream" ]; then
            local commit bin_rel build_cmd build_cwd
            commit="$(manifest_get "$manifest" .commit)"
            bin_rel="$(manifest_get "$manifest" .binary_src)"
            build_cmd="$(manifest_get "$manifest" .build_cmd)"
            build_cwd="$(manifest_get "$manifest" '.build_cwd // "."')"
            echo "  upstream: $upstream (commit $commit)"
            local candidate="$upstream/$bin_rel"
            if [ -f "$candidate" ]; then
                cor24_src="$candidate"
            else
                echo "  warning: $candidate not built" >&2
                echo "           run '$build_cmd' in $upstream/$build_cwd" >&2
            fi
        fi
    fi

    if [ -z "$cor24_src" ]; then
        local sys_bin
        sys_bin="$(command -v cor24-run 2>/dev/null || true)"
        if [ -n "$sys_bin" ]; then
            cor24_src="$sys_bin"
            echo "  source: system PATH ($cor24_src)"
        fi
    fi

    if [ -z "$cor24_src" ]; then
        echo "error: sw-em24: no cor24-run binary could be resolved" >&2
        echo "  tried (in order):" >&2
        echo "    1. \$SW_EM24_BIN override     -- unset" >&2
        echo "    2. $expected_path/target/release/cor24-run" >&2
        echo "                                  -- sibling not populated or not built" >&2
        echo "    3. cor24-run on system PATH   -- not found" >&2
        echo "  to fix, pick one:" >&2
        echo "    - export SW_EM24_BIN=/path/to/cor24-run  (or set it in vendor/active.env)" >&2
        echo "    - git clone https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator.git $REPO_ROOT/$expected_path" >&2
        echo "      && (cd $REPO_ROOT/$expected_path && cargo build --release)" >&2
        return 1
    fi

    cp "$cor24_src" "$dest/bin/cor24-run"
    chmod +x "$dest/bin/cor24-run"
    echo "  copied: cor24-run"
}

# --- Main -------------------------------------------------------------------

echo "vendor-fetch: mode=$MODE"
echo ""

fetch_em24
fetch_em24_rc=$?
echo ""

echo "vendor-fetch: done"
exit "$fetch_em24_rc"

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# build.sh -- Assemble src/sw-as24.s into build/sw-as24.bin using
# the vendored cor24-run. Also emits a listing (build/sw-as24.lst)
# for debugging.
#
# cor24-run --assemble takes three positional arguments: the input
# .s file, the output .bin file, and the output .lst file. That
# invocation is the one the sw-cor24-ocaml build.sh uses and is the
# reference for this project.
#
# Usage: ./scripts/build.sh

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"

# --- Load vendored tool versions -------------------------------------------

ACTIVE_ENV="$REPO_ROOT/vendor/active.env"
if [ ! -f "$ACTIVE_ENV" ]; then
    echo "error: $ACTIVE_ENV not found" >&2
    echo "hint: ensure vendor/ is initialised (step 003-vendor-manifest)" >&2
    exit 4
fi
# shellcheck source=/dev/null
. "$ACTIVE_ENV"

# --- Resolve cor24-run -----------------------------------------------------

COR24_RUN="$REPO_ROOT/vendor/sw-em24/${SW_EM24_VERSION:?SW_EM24_VERSION unset}/bin/cor24-run"
if [ ! -x "$COR24_RUN" ]; then
    echo "error: cor24-run not found at $COR24_RUN" >&2
    echo "hint: run 'just vendor-fetch' to materialise it from the" >&2
    echo "      sibling sw-cor24-emulator repo" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# --- Paths -----------------------------------------------------------------

SRC="$REPO_ROOT/src/sw-as24.s"
BUILD_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/build"
BIN="$BUILD_DIR/sw-as24.bin"
LST="$BUILD_DIR/sw-as24.lst"

if [ ! -f "$SRC" ]; then
    echo "error: $SRC not found" >&2
    echo "hint: step 008-sw-as24-skeleton writes this file" >&2
    exit 1
fi

mkdir -p "$BUILD_DIR"

# --- Assemble -------------------------------------------------------------

echo "assembling $SRC"
echo "  -> $BIN"
echo "  -> $LST"
# cor24-run --assemble prints `Assembly error: ...` to stdout and
# exits 0 even on errors. Capture stdout, surface it, and fail if
# any error line is present.
ASM_OUTPUT="$("$COR24_RUN" --assemble "$SRC" "$BIN" "$LST" 2>&1)"
if [ -n "$ASM_OUTPUT" ]; then
    echo "$ASM_OUTPUT"
fi
if echo "$ASM_OUTPUT" | grep -q -i '^\(assembly \)\?error\|^error:'; then
    echo "build: FAILED (assembly errors above)" >&2
    exit 1
fi
echo "build: done"

content.text:
# Moving from cor24-rs to sw-cor24-x-assembler (for assembly only)

## What sw-cor24-x-assembler actually is

- `crate-type = ["rlib"]` — library only. **No binary**, no CLI, no `--assemble` entry point.
- Cargo dep on `../sw-cor24-emulator` (sw-embed fork) for `cpu::encode` + `Opcode`.
- API: `Assembler::new() / assemble(src) -> AssemblyResult { bytes, lines, errors, labels }`. `AssembledLine` has address + bytes + source + label.
- Implements a two-pass assembler with labels, forward refs, directives, relative/absolute addressing. Feature parity with cor24-run looks close but is not identical source.
- Not present at the usual `sw-embed/` sibling slot in this tree — lives under `dcxas/github/sw-embed/`. `sw-cor24-emulator` isn't checked out locally at all.

## Gaps vs. today's vendor pipeline

The current flow (`vendor-fetch.sh` → `build.sh`) needs one thing: a binary named `cor24-run` that accepts `--assemble <in.s> <out.bin> <out.lst>`. x-assembler provides none of that.

1. **Wrapper bin required.** Need a small CLI crate that: opens the input, calls `Assembler::assemble_at`, writes `.bin`, formats and writes `.lst` matching cor24-run's layout, exits nonzero on errors. Either a `[[bin]]` target in x-assembler upstream or a new thin crate in this repo. Tens of lines, but it's real new code that has to be owned somewhere.
2. **Two siblings to vendor, not one.** x-assembler's path dep forces vendor-fetch to also resolve `sw-cor24-emulator` (currently uncloned locally). The manifest scheme is already plural-capable, so this is schema work + a second version.json, not a rewrite.
3. **Byte-identical invariant becomes fragile.** CLAUDE.md's hard invariant is "byte-identical to `cor24-run --assemble`". If x-assembler replaces cor24-run as the *producer*, the oracle shifts. Before cutover you'd need to prove x-assembler(nop.s) == cor24-run(nop.s) — and for every `.s` in use, including sw-as24.s once it exists. Any divergence forces a choice: fix x-assembler, fix the invariant wording, or keep cor24-run as the reference and only use x-assembler behind it.
4. **Listing format.** `.lst` output has a specific shape today. `AssemblyResult::lines` gives you the data but not the format; the wrapper has to reproduce cor24-run's listing verbatim if anything downstream reads it (quick audit: smoke test only diffs `.bin`, so listing format is a soft requirement).
5. **Error surfacing is better.** x-assembler returns `errors: Vec<String>` — the wrapper can exit nonzero, dropping the stdout-grep hack in `build.sh`. Small cleanup win.
6. **`just run FILE` still needs cor24-run.** x-assembler doesn't emulate. You'd be running a split toolchain: x-assembler for `build`/`test-vs-oracle`, cor24-run for `run`. That's fine but means you vendor *both* long-term, not drop one.

## Rough sizing

- Wrapper CLI: half-day, plus whatever listing-format fidelity demands.
- Equivalence audit (x-assembler ≡ cor24-run on real sources): half-day, and potentially opens a rabbit hole if they disagree.
- Vendor plumbing (second manifest, vendor-fetch extension, active.env bump): half-day.
- Doc churn (CLAUDE.md, prd.md, architecture.md, design.md, plan.md references to "cor24-run --assemble"): half-day.

## The real question

What's the motivation? If it's "get off cor24-rs because sw-embed owns x-assembler," the byte-identity audit is the blocker — those two codebases aren't the same assembler, just nearby ones. If it's "cleaner dep graph," a cheaper path is a `[[bin]]` target added upstream to x-assembler so vendor-fetch keeps fetching one tool. Worth deciding before any code moves.

message.content: confused.  cor24-rs was an earlier project that combined assembler and emulator (and loader?).  I'm trying to split this into separte projects. the emulator, the cross-assembler, and other tools.  The current directory is a future project to self-host the same "kind" of assembler, but implemented in cor24 .s instead of Rust.  Note that this all started with makerlisp.com sending me as24.c -- a gcc-based assembler for his COR24 ISA.  My cor24-rs has a Rust-based bug-compatible clean-room clone of this.  But it does too much, combining emulator, assembler, loader, etc.  I want separate repos.  I want to eventually self-host the assembler to run on an FPGA board (not tied to an emulator).  Does this clarify?

content.text:
Yes, that reframes it. x-assembler isn't a sideways swap — it's the **canonical successor** to cor24-rs's assembler portion. Several of my earlier concerns dissolve or invert.

## What changes with this framing

- **Byte-identity audit is less of a blocker, more of a baseline.** If x-assembler was carved from cor24-rs's assembler (worth confirming — clean-room vs. extraction), they should agree by construction. The real question becomes: as cor24-rs deprecates, x-assembler *is* the oracle. CLAUDE.md's invariant wording needs to repoint, not be defended.
- **Split toolchain is the target, not a compromise.** x-assembler for `build`, sw-cor24-emulator for `run`. `just run` should eventually invoke the emulator repo's binary, not cor24-rs. This repo is where "uses cor24-rs for both" gets retired.
- **Wrapper CLI belongs upstream.** A `[[bin]]` target on x-assembler (as24-equivalent CLI: `as24 in.s out.bin out.lst`) is the Right Place — every cross-assembler has one, makerlisp's as24.c *is* one. Don't ship a wrapper in this repo; push it to x-assembler. Then vendor-fetch here pulls a binary by the same mechanism as today.

## The coupling worth flagging

x-assembler's Cargo.toml says `cor24-emulator = { path = "../sw-cor24-emulator" }`, and the code does `use cor24_emulator::cpu::encode;` + `use cor24_emulator::cpu::instruction::Opcode;`. So the **assembler depends on the emulator for ISA tables**. For your "separate projects, FPGA target, no emulator dep" goal, that's backwards — the encoding tables are the assembler's data, not the emulator's. Emulator should depend on a shared `cor24-isa` crate (or on x-assembler's tables), not the other way round. Worth untangling before x-assembler stabilizes, because every consumer (this repo included) inherits that dep.

## Concrete cutover checklist

1. Upstream a `[[bin]]` to x-assembler that matches `as24 in.s out.bin out.lst` semantics + nonzero exit on errors.
2. Decide on the ISA-tables coupling direction (above) — ideally before pinning x-assembler here.
3. Add a `vendor/sw-xas24/<v>/version.json` pointing at `../sw-cor24-x-assembler`; `vendor-fetch.sh` grows a second fetch stanza.
4. Switch `build.sh` to call the x-assembler binary instead of `cor24-run --assemble`.
5. Replace `cor24-run` with the emulator repo's binary for `just run` / `just test` (separate vendored tool — `sw-em24` manifest repoints from cor24-rs to sw-cor24-emulator, which is already half-signalled in the existing manifest `notes`).
6. Retire cor24-rs from this repo's vendor set entirely. Docs (CLAUDE.md, prd.md, architecture.md, design.md) repoint to the two successor repos.

Order matters: (1) and (2) are upstream work in other repos. (3)–(6) are local and cheap once those land.

message.content: reframe: as24.c also has a companion op-code table for the FPGA. I have a REST API to run as24.c against .s inputs to produce .lst outputs for compatibility testing of any assembler (I used this to develop the Rust version).  A lot of what you described seems related to other repos, not this repo.  This repo needs to (a) "know" the ISA (op codes, register descriptions, etc.) and all that exists in other repos (cor24-rs, sw-cor24-x-assembler, ...emulator..., etc.).  We can copy that architecture documentation here.  We can use the REST service in our function testing to ensure compatibility.  We can use one of the emulators to run assembled programs (part of our testing).  However, for the self-hosting, this assembler needs to run on COR24 FPGA with a monitor and simple scripting tools to edit, assemble, link, load, and run (with no REST server, no Rust, no gcc).  So what do we need to document in this repo?  What is missing for this repo.  Until we can assemble ourself, I prefer that we use the newest Rust-based assembler (the x-assembler one, not the cor24-rs one which I am trying to retire).

input.prompt:
Scan the docs in /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/dcasm/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-assembler/docs/ (architecture.md, design.md, plan.md, prd.md, utility.md) and also src/sw-as24.s and tests/smoke/nop.s. I need to know:

1. **ISA coverage**: Do these docs contain authoritative ISA documentation for COR24 — opcode tables, instruction encoding, register descriptions, addressing modes, instruction formats? Or do they just REFERENCE the ISA as defined elsewhere (sibling repos, cor24-rs, etc.)?

2. **Assembler language / grammar spec**: Is there a spec for what the .s syntax accepts — directives (.word, .org, .byte, etc.), label rules, literal forms, comment conventions, case sensitivity?

3. **Output format spec**: Is there a spec for the .bin format (byte layout) and .lst listing format produced by the assembler?

4. **FPGA target / runtime environment**: Is there any spec of the FPGA monitor, memory map, UART protocol, load format, entry-point conventions — i.e. the runtime environment where the self-hosted assembler will actually run?

5. **Monitor / scripting tooling**: Any docs describing a COR24-resident monitor, editor, linker, loader, or runner — the "edit, assemble, link, load, run" stack the self-hosted assembler needs to live alongside?

6. **Compatibility testing / REST oracle**: Any docs describing how to compare against a reference (REST service running as24.c, byte-identical oracle, test corpus)?

7. **Vendor/bootstrap assembler**: What does each doc currently name as the bootstrap cross-assembler? Is it cor24-rs, sw-cor24-x-assembler, or something else?

For each of the 7 questions, report: which file(s) cover it, how completely (brief / partial / thorough / absent), and quote 1-2 sentences of the strongest relevant passage if present. Report in under 500 words — I need a gap inventory, not a comprehensive summary.

content.text:
Based on my scan of the documentation and source files, here is the gap inventory across the 7 questions:

## 1. ISA Coverage
**Files:** architecture.md (Section 2, 5), plan.md  
**Coverage:** Brief/reference-only  
**Key quote:** "Encoding tables (opcodes, register numbers, ISA types) originate in `sw-cor24-emulator`'s decode ROM; see `../sw-cor24-emulator` for the authoritative definitions."  
**Gap:** No opcode tables, instruction formats, or register descriptions exist here. Documentation explicitly defers to sibling repos (cor24-emulator, cor24-rs).

## 2. Assembler Language / Grammar Spec
**Files:** architecture.md (Section 4–5), design.md, plan.md  
**Coverage:** Partial  
**Key quote:** "All mnemonics, addressing modes, and directives accepted by the Rust cross-assembler." (architecture.md 5). Comments covered: "; to end of line" (architecture.md 3).  
**Gap:** No formal grammar, no spec for label syntax, literal forms, case sensitivity, directive syntax, or whitespace rules. These are left for later sagas (saga 2+ per plan.md).

## 3. Output Format Spec
**Files:** design.md (Section "Smoke test"), utility.md  
**Coverage:** Partial (hex-ASCII only)  
**Key quote:** "sw-as24 emits hex-ASCII on UART TX rather than raw bytes... scripts/hex2bin.sh is the host-side decoder" (design.md).  
**Gap:** No spec for `.bin` binary byte layout, no `.lst` listing format documented. Hex-ASCII output is described as a transport layer; actual `.bin` format unspecified.

## 4. FPGA Target / Runtime Environment
**Files:** architecture.md (Section 6), design.md (UART/FPGA references), src/sw-as24.s  
**Coverage:** Minimal  
**Key quote:** "Two options under consideration... (i) Standalone, UART-only... (ii) Monitor-hosted, service-vector I/O" (architecture.md 6).  
**Gap:** No memory map, FPGA monitor spec, entry-point conventions, or load format documented. Hosting model is explicitly "TBD" and deferred to saga 11 (plan.md).

## 5. Monitor / Scripting Tooling
**Files:** architecture.md (Section 6), utility.md, plan.md (saga 11)  
**Coverage:** Forward-looking sketch only  
**Key quote:** "Eventually (hosting-model dependent): `sw-cor24-monitor` service vectors" (architecture.md 7). Utility.md describes a future `sw-hexload` paired with an eventual monitor.  
**Gap:** No monitor, editor, linker, loader, or runner documented. These are explicitly out-of-scope for relaunch (saga 1) and deferred to later sagas.

## 6. Compatibility Testing / REST Oracle
**Files:** architecture.md (Section 9), prd.md (Assumptions A1)  
**Coverage:** Abstract contract only  
**Key quote:** "sw-as24's output is contractually byte-identical to cor24-run's output for every input both tools accept. This is the one invariant the regression suite defends."  
**Gap:** No REST service, oracle spec, or test corpus documented. Compatibility is enforced via `just test` (smoke test only today; full corpus regression deferred to saga 12, plan.md).

## 7. Vendor / Bootstrap Assembler
**Files:** architecture.md (Section 2), design.md (Vendor strategy), prd.md (A2), plan.md (saga 1)  
**Coverage:** Thorough  
**Key quote:** "During development, the vendored `cor24-run` both assembles `sw-as24.s` (acting as the cross-assembler) and executes the resulting binary."  
**Bootstrap name:** Rust cross-assembler via `cor24-run` (emulator binary), part of `sw-cor24-x-assembler` library in `cor24-rs` upstream. Pinned to commit `40033d90e80dcef1a420bd3db7c8fd22fb9f181f` (plan.md, saga 1, step 5).

---

**Summary:** ISA, language grammar, output formats, FPGA runtime, monitor/tooling, and oracle specs are all either absent or forward-references to sibling repos. The relaunch saga deliberately minimizes scope: docs define process and invariants, not ISA details. Actual ISA data lives in `cor24-rs` and `sw-cor24-emulator`. Full grammar, two-pass assembly, directives, and self-hosting testing are sagas 2–13.

## 5. Monitor / Scripting Tooling
**Files:** architecture.md (Section 6), utility.md, plan.md (saga 11)  
**Coverage:** Forward-looking sketch only  
**Key quote:** "Eventually (hosting-model dependent): `sw-cor24-monitor` service vectors" (architecture.md 7). Utility.md describes a future `sw-hexload` paired with an eventual monitor.  
**Gap:** No monitor, editor, linker, loader, or runner documented. These are explicitly out-of-scope for relaunch (saga 1) and deferred to later sagas.

## 6. Compatibility Testing / REST Oracle
**Files:** architecture.md (Section 9), prd.md (Assumptions A1)  
**Coverage:** Abstract contract only  
**Key quote:** "sw-as24's output is contractually byte-identical to cor24-run's output for every input both tools accept. This is the one invariant the regression suite defends."  
**Gap:** No REST service, oracle spec, or test corpus documented. Compatibility is enforced via `just test` (smoke test only today; full corpus regression deferred to saga 12, plan.md).

## 7. Vendor / Bootstrap Assembler
**Files:** architecture.md (Section 2), design.md (Vendor strategy), prd.md (A2), plan.md (saga 1)  
**Coverage:** Thorough  
**Key quote:** "During development, the vendored `cor24-run` both assembles `sw-as24.s` (acting as the cross-assembler) and executes the resulting binary."  
**Bootstrap name:** Rust cross-assembler via `cor24-run` (emulator binary), part of `sw-cor24-x-assembler` library in `cor24-rs` upstream. Pinned to commit `40033d90e80dcef1a420bd3db7c8fd22fb9f181f` (plan.md, saga 1, step 5).

---

**Summary:** ISA, language grammar, output formats, FPGA runtime, monitor/tooling, and oracle specs are all either absent or forward-references to sibling repos. The relaunch saga deliberately minimizes scope: docs define process and invariants, not ISA details. Actual ISA data lives in `cor24-rs` and `sw-cor24-emulator`. Full grammar, two-pass assembly, directives, and self-hosting testing are sagas 2–13.

Understood — scope is this repo's self-sufficiency, not the decomposition of the others. The scan confirms: almost all ISA/grammar/runtime content today is a *pointer* to sibling repos. For the FPGA-resident self-host endgame (no Rust, no REST, no gcc on the target), those pointers break. This repo has to become authoritative.

## What should live here (copied, not referenced)

1. **ISA reference.** Opcode table, instruction formats (1/2/4-byte encodings), register roles (r0–r2 GP; r3=fp, r4=sp, r5=z, r6=iv, r7=ir), addressing modes, condition-flag semantics. Today: one line saying "see `../sw-cor24-emulator`." Gap: total. This is data, not prose — a table, probably generated once from the decode ROM and then frozen here.
2. **Assembler language spec.** Exactly what `.s` syntax the self-hosted assembler must accept: mnemonics, operand forms, literal syntax (decimal/hex/char/string), directives (`.org`, `.word`, `.byte`, `.string`, whatever else as24.c accepts), label rules ("on their own line" per x-assembler today), comment conventions (`;` to EOL), case sensitivity, whitespace. Today: scattered sentences. Gap: no grammar, no directive inventory.
3. **Output formats.** `.bin` byte layout (raw image? base address? padding?), `.lst` listing format (columns, address width, byte grouping, label column). Today: hex-ASCII UART transport is documented; the artifact formats themselves are not. Gap: needed because `just test` is a byte diff — the format is implicitly the oracle's, and the oracle is about to move.
4. **FPGA runtime spec.** Memory map, reset vector, UART I/O contract, entry point, load address, stack placement, interrupt vector table. Today: architecture.md names two hosting options and marks it TBD. Gap: everything the assembler has to assume about its host.
5. **Monitor / tooling contract.** The "edit, assemble, link, load, run" stack you described lives adjacent to sw-as24 and probably in this repo (or a sibling this repo coordinates with). Needed: who owns the editor? the loader (sw-hexload in utility.md is a sketch)? the linker (is there one, or is sw-as24 direct-to-image)? the `run` command on the board? Today: one section sketches options; nothing decided.
6. **Compatibility oracle protocol.** Spec for the REST service: endpoint, request shape, response shape, how to run a corpus diff against it, where the corpus lives. Today: absent — the invariant is stated abstractly ("byte-identical to cor24-run"), but there's no procedure and no corpus beyond `nop.s`.
7. **Compatibility corpus.** A set of `.s` fixtures that collectively exercise every opcode, every directive, every addressing mode. Today: one `nop.s`. Gap: needed so the REST oracle has something to chew on, and so self-host verification means something.

## What's not missing (but needs updating)

- **Bootstrap assembler pointer.** Currently cor24-rs. Repoint to x-assembler — small vendor edit: swap `vendor/sw-em24/` for `vendor/sw-xas24/`, point `repo_path_local` at `../sw-cor24-x-assembler`, update `build.sh` to call its CLI (assuming a `[[bin]]` lands upstream — if not, this blocks). Emulator for `just run` stays a separate vendored tool.
- **Test oracle wording.** "byte-identical to `cor24-run --assemble`" becomes "byte-identical to x-assembler's CLI" — or, better, "byte-identical to the REST oracle" since that's the most durable reference (and outlives both Rust tools).

## Suggested order

1. Repoint vendor to x-assembler (unblocks today's workflow, retires cor24-rs from this repo).
2. Copy the ISA reference in as a frozen doc (item 1). Biggest content gap, blocks everything downstream.
3. Write the language spec (item 2) — this is what sw-as24.s actually implements; the spec and the source should be developed together.
4. Define the REST oracle protocol + seed the corpus (items 6–7). Gives you a real regression signal beyond `nop.s`.
5. FPGA runtime + tooling (items 4–5) — can wait until the assembler is mature enough to target something, but the hosting decision gates self-hosting.

Items 3–5 are roughly the "sagas 2–13" territory already sketched in plan.md; the framing shift is that their deliverables must be *documents owned by this repo*, not references out.

content.content:
1	# COR24 ISA Reference
2	
3	C-Oriented RISC 24-bit Instruction Set Architecture
4	
5	## Overview
6	
7	COR24 is a 24-bit RISC architecture designed for efficient C code execution. It features:
8	
9	- **24-bit data path** - Registers and addresses are 24 bits wide
10	- **3 general-purpose registers** - r0, r1, r2
11	- **5 special-purpose registers** - fp, sp, z, iv, ir
12	- **Single condition flag** - C (carry/condition)
13	- **Variable-length instructions** - 1, 2, or 4 bytes
14	- **16MB address space** - Full 24-bit addressing
15	- **Little-endian** byte ordering
16	- **Memory-mapped I/O**
17	
18	## Registers
19	
20	| Register | Name | Description |
21	|----------|------|-------------|
22	| r0 | - | General purpose / return value |
23	| r1 | - | Return address (link register) |
24	| r2 | - | General purpose |
25	| r3 | fp | Frame pointer |
26	| r4 | sp | Stack pointer |
27	| r5 | z | Zero (for compare instructions) |
28	| r6 | - | Interrupt vector |
29	| r7 | - | Used for absolute jumps |
30	| PC | - | Program counter (24-bit) |
31	| C | - | Condition flag (1-bit) |
32	
33	## Memory Map
34	
35	| Address Range | Description |
36	|---------------|-------------|
37	| 0x000000 - 0xFDFFFF | SRAM (external memory) |
38	| 0xFE0000 - 0xFEFFFF | Embedded block RAM (fast) |
39	| 0xFF0000 - 0xFFFFFF | Memory-mapped I/O |
40	
41	The CPU starts execution at address 0xFEE000 on reset.
42	
43	## Instruction Set Summary
44	
45	### Arithmetic Instructions
46	
47	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
48	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
49	| `add ra,rb` | 1 | 0x00 | ra = ra + rb |
50	| `add ra,dd` | 2 | 0x01 | ra = ra + sign_extend(dd) |
51	| `sub ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1A | ra = ra - rb |
52	| `sub sp,dddddd` | 4 | 0x1B | sp = sp - dddddd (24-bit) |
53	| `mul ra,rb` | 1 | 0x12 | ra = ra * rb (lower 24 bits) |
54	
55	### Logical Instructions
56	
57	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
58	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
59	| `and ra,rb` | 1 | 0x02 | ra = ra & rb |
60	| `or ra,rb` | 1 | 0x13 | ra = ra \| rb |
61	| `xor ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1E | ra = ra ^ rb |
62	
63	### Shift Instructions
64	
65	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
66	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
67	| `shl ra,rb` | 1 | 0x17 | ra = ra << rb[4:0] |
68	| `sra ra,rb` | 1 | 0x18 | ra = ra >> rb[4:0] (arithmetic) |
69	| `srl ra,rb` | 1 | 0x19 | ra = ra >> rb[4:0] (logical) |
70	
71	### Compare Instructions
72	
73	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
74	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
75	| `ceq ra,rb` | 1 | 0x06 | C = (ra == rb) |
76	| `cls ra,rb` | 1 | 0x07 | C = (ra < rb) signed |
77	| `clu ra,rb` | 1 | 0x08 | C = (ra < rb) unsigned |
78	
79	### Branch Instructions
80	
81	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
82	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
83	| `bra dd` | 2 | 0x03 | PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
84	| `brf dd` | 2 | 0x04 | if (!C) PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
85	| `brt dd` | 2 | 0x05 | if (C) PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
86	
87	### Jump Instructions
88	
89	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
90	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
91	| `jmp (ra)` | 1 | 0x0A | PC = ra |
92	| `jal ra,(rb)` | 1 | 0x09 | ra = PC + 1; PC = rb |
93	
94	### Load Instructions
95	
96	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
97	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
98	| `la ra,dddddd` | 4 | 0x0B | ra = dddddd (24-bit address) |
99	| `lb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x0C | ra = sign_extend(mem[rb + dd]) |
100	| `lbu ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x0D | ra = zero_extend(mem[rb + dd]) |
101	| `lc ra,dd` | 2 | 0x0E | ra = sign_extend(dd) |
102	| `lcu ra,dd` | 2 | 0x0F | ra = zero_extend(dd) |
103	| `lw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x10 | ra = mem24[rb + dd] |
104	
105	### Store Instructions
106	
107	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
108	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
109	| `sb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x16 | mem[rb + dd] = ra[7:0] |
110	| `sw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x1C | mem24[rb + dd] = ra |
111	
112	### Move Instructions
113	
114	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
115	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
116	| `mov ra,rb` | 1 | 0x11 | ra = rb |
117	| `mov ra,c` | 1 | 0x11 | ra = C (when rb=5) |
118	| `sxt ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1D | ra = sign_extend(rb[7:0]) |
119	| `zxt ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1F | ra = zero_extend(rb[7:0]) |
120	
121	### Stack Instructions
122	
123	| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
124	|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
125	| `push ra` | 1 | 0x15 | sp -= 3; mem24[sp] = ra |
126	| `pop ra` | 1 | 0x14 | ra = mem24[sp]; sp += 3 |
127	
128	## Instruction Encoding
129	
130	Instructions are encoded with a lookup ROM that maps 8-bit opcodes to decoded operations. The instruction byte encodes the operation and register operands together.
131	
132	### Single-Byte Instructions (Register Operations)
133	
134	| Byte | Instruction |
135	|------|-------------|
136	| 0x01 | add r0,r1 |
137	| 0x02 | add r0,r2 |
138	| 0x1A | cls r0,r2 |
139	| 0x1B | cls r1,r0 |
140	| 0x25 | jal r1,(r0) |
141	| 0x27 | jmp (r1) |
142	| 0x57 | mov r0,r2 |
143	| 0x65 | mov fp,sp |
144	| 0x69 | mov sp,fp |
145	| 0x7A | pop r1 |
146	| 0x7B | pop r2 |
147	| 0x7C | pop fp |
148	| 0x7D | push r0 |
149	| 0x7E | push r1 |
150	| 0x7F | push r2 |
151	| 0x80 | push fp |
152	| 0xCE | clu z,r0 |
153	
154	### Two-Byte Instructions (Register + Immediate)
155	
156	| First Byte | Instruction Format |
157	|------------|-------------------|
158	| 0x09 | add r0,imm8 |
159	| 0x0B | add r2,imm8 |
160	| 0x0C | add sp,imm8 |
161	| 0x13 | bra offset8 |
162	| 0x14 | brf offset8 |
163	| 0x15 | brt offset8 |
164	| 0x2C | lb r0,(r0) with offset |
165	| 0x2E | lb r0,(r2) with offset |
166	| 0x44 | lc r0,imm8 |
167	| 0x45 | lc r1,imm8 |
168	| 0x46 | lc r2,imm8 |
169	| 0x4D | lw r0,offset(fp) |
170	| 0x51 | lw r1,offset(fp) |
171	| 0x55 | lw r2,offset(fp) |
172	| 0x82 | sb r0,(r2) with offset |
173	| 0x84 | sb r1,(r0) with offset |
174	| 0xA6 | sw r0,offset(fp) |
175	
176	### Four-Byte Instructions (Register + 24-bit Address)
177	
178	| First Byte | Instruction Format |
179	|------------|-------------------|
180	| 0x29 | la r0,addr24 |
181	| 0x2A | la r1,addr24 |
182	| 0x2B | la r2,addr24 |
183	
184	## Calling Convention
185	
186	### Function Prologue
187	```asm
188	push    fp          ; Save frame pointer
189	push    r2          ; Save callee-saved register
190	push    r1          ; Save return address
191	mov     fp,sp       ; Set up frame pointer
192	add     sp,-N       ; Allocate stack space
193	```
194	
195	### Function Epilogue
196	```asm
197	mov     sp,fp       ; Restore stack pointer
198	pop     r1          ; Restore return address
199	pop     r2          ; Restore callee-saved register
200	pop     fp          ; Restore frame pointer
201	jmp     (r1)        ; Return
202	```
203	
204	### Argument Passing
205	- Arguments are pushed right-to-left onto the stack
206	- Return value is in r0
207	- Caller cleans up arguments with `add sp,N`
208	
209	### Stack Frame Layout
210	```
211	Higher addresses
212	+----------------+
213	| Argument N     | fp + (N*3 + 6)
214	| ...            |
215	| Argument 1     | fp + 9
216	+----------------+
217	| Saved r1       | fp + 6
218	| Saved r2       | fp + 3
219	| Saved fp       | fp + 0
220	+----------------+
221	| Local var 1    | fp - 3
222	| Local var 2    | fp - 6
223	| ...            |
224	+----------------+
225	Lower addresses (sp)
226	```
227	
228	## Word Size
229	
230	- Words are 24 bits (3 bytes)
231	- Stack operations push/pop 3 bytes at a time
232	- Word addresses should be 3-byte aligned for efficiency
233	
234	## Example: Fibonacci
235	
236	```asm
237	_fib:
238	        push    fp              ; Save frame pointer
239	        push    r2              ; Save r2
240	        push    r1              ; Save return address
241	        mov     fp,sp           ; Set up frame
242	        add     sp,-3           ; Local variable space
243	        lw      r2,9(fp)        ; Load argument n
244	
245	        lc      r0,2            ; Load constant 2
246	        cls     r2,r0           ; Compare n < 2
247	        brf     L17             ; Branch if false
248	
249	        lc      r0,1            ; Return 1
250	        bra     L16             ; Jump to epilogue
251	
252	L17:
253	        mov     r0,r2           ; r0 = n
254	        add     r0,-1           ; r0 = n - 1
255	        push    r0              ; Push argument
256	        la      r0,_fib         ; Load fib address
257	        jal     r1,(r0)         ; Call fib(n-1)
258	        add     sp,3            ; Clean up argument
259	        sw      r0,-3(fp)       ; Save result
260	
261	        mov     r0,r2           ; r0 = n
262	        add     r0,-2           ; r0 = n - 2
263	        push    r0              ; Push argument
264	        la      r0,_fib         ; Load fib address
265	        jal     r1,(r0)         ; Call fib(n-2)
266	        add     sp,3            ; Clean up argument
267	        lw      r1,-3(fp)       ; Load fib(n-1)
268	        add     r0,r1           ; r0 = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
269	
270	L16:
271	        mov     sp,fp           ; Restore stack
272	        pop     r1              ; Restore return address
273	        pop     r2              ; Restore r2
274	        pop     fp              ; Restore frame pointer
275	        jmp     (r1)            ; Return
276	```
277	
278	## Interrupts
279	
280	- Interrupt vector is in r6
281	- When an interrupt occurs, `jal r7,(r6)` is executed
282	- Return from interrupt with `jmp (r7)`
283	- Interrupts can be disabled by the `intis` flag
284	
285	## References
286	
287	- Verilog source: `cor24_cpu.v`
288	- Example programs: `demo/*.lst`
289	- Hardware manual: `doc/COR24-TB-MAN.pdf`
290	- MakerLisp: https://makerlisp.com
291	
292	## License
293	
294	The COR24 CPU is open-source under the MIT License.
295

# COR24 ISA Reference

C-Oriented RISC 24-bit Instruction Set Architecture

## Overview

COR24 is a 24-bit RISC architecture designed for efficient C code execution. It features:

- **24-bit data path** - Registers and addresses are 24 bits wide
- **3 general-purpose registers** - r0, r1, r2
- **5 special-purpose registers** - fp, sp, z, iv, ir
- **Single condition flag** - C (carry/condition)
- **Variable-length instructions** - 1, 2, or 4 bytes
- **16MB address space** - Full 24-bit addressing
- **Little-endian** byte ordering
- **Memory-mapped I/O**

## Registers

| Register | Name | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| r0 | - | General purpose / return value |
| r1 | - | Return address (link register) |
| r2 | - | General purpose |
| r3 | fp | Frame pointer |
| r4 | sp | Stack pointer |
| r5 | z | Zero (for compare instructions) |
| r6 | - | Interrupt vector |
| r7 | - | Used for absolute jumps |
| PC | - | Program counter (24-bit) |
| C | - | Condition flag (1-bit) |

## Memory Map

| Address Range | Description |
|---------------|-------------|
| 0x000000 - 0xFDFFFF | SRAM (external memory) |
| 0xFE0000 - 0xFEFFFF | Embedded block RAM (fast) |
| 0xFF0000 - 0xFFFFFF | Memory-mapped I/O |

The CPU starts execution at address 0xFEE000 on reset.

## Instruction Set Summary

### Arithmetic Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `add ra,rb` | 1 | 0x00 | ra = ra + rb |
| `add ra,dd` | 2 | 0x01 | ra = ra + sign_extend(dd) |
| `sub ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1A | ra = ra - rb |
| `sub sp,dddddd` | 4 | 0x1B | sp = sp - dddddd (24-bit) |
| `mul ra,rb` | 1 | 0x12 | ra = ra * rb (lower 24 bits) |

### Logical Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `and ra,rb` | 1 | 0x02 | ra = ra & rb |
| `or ra,rb` | 1 | 0x13 | ra = ra \| rb |
| `xor ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1E | ra = ra ^ rb |

### Shift Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `shl ra,rb` | 1 | 0x17 | ra = ra << rb[4:0] |
| `sra ra,rb` | 1 | 0x18 | ra = ra >> rb[4:0] (arithmetic) |
| `srl ra,rb` | 1 | 0x19 | ra = ra >> rb[4:0] (logical) |

### Compare Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `ceq ra,rb` | 1 | 0x06 | C = (ra == rb) |
| `cls ra,rb` | 1 | 0x07 | C = (ra < rb) signed |
| `clu ra,rb` | 1 | 0x08 | C = (ra < rb) unsigned |

### Branch Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `bra dd` | 2 | 0x03 | PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
| `brf dd` | 2 | 0x04 | if (!C) PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
| `brt dd` | 2 | 0x05 | if (C) PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |

### Jump Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `jmp (ra)` | 1 | 0x0A | PC = ra |
| `jal ra,(rb)` | 1 | 0x09 | ra = PC + 1; PC = rb |

### Load Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `la ra,dddddd` | 4 | 0x0B | ra = dddddd (24-bit address) |
| `lb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x0C | ra = sign_extend(mem[rb + dd]) |
| `lbu ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x0D | ra = zero_extend(mem[rb + dd]) |
| `lc ra,dd` | 2 | 0x0E | ra = sign_extend(dd) |
| `lcu ra,dd` | 2 | 0x0F | ra = zero_extend(dd) |
| `lw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x10 | ra = mem24[rb + dd] |

### Store Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `sb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x16 | mem[rb + dd] = ra[7:0] |
| `sw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x1C | mem24[rb + dd] = ra |

### Move Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `mov ra,rb` | 1 | 0x11 | ra = rb |
| `mov ra,c` | 1 | 0x11 | ra = C (when rb=5) |
| `sxt ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1D | ra = sign_extend(rb[7:0]) |
| `zxt ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1F | ra = zero_extend(rb[7:0]) |

### Stack Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `push ra` | 1 | 0x15 | sp -= 3; mem24[sp] = ra |
| `pop ra` | 1 | 0x14 | ra = mem24[sp]; sp += 3 |

## Instruction Encoding

Instructions are encoded with a lookup ROM that maps 8-bit opcodes to decoded operations. The instruction byte encodes the operation and register operands together.

### Single-Byte Instructions (Register Operations)

| Byte | Instruction |
|------|-------------|
| 0x01 | add r0,r1 |
| 0x02 | add r0,r2 |
| 0x1A | cls r0,r2 |
| 0x1B | cls r1,r0 |
| 0x25 | jal r1,(r0) |
| 0x27 | jmp (r1) |
| 0x57 | mov r0,r2 |
| 0x65 | mov fp,sp |
| 0x69 | mov sp,fp |
| 0x7A | pop r1 |
| 0x7B | pop r2 |
| 0x7C | pop fp |
| 0x7D | push r0 |
| 0x7E | push r1 |
| 0x7F | push r2 |
| 0x80 | push fp |
| 0xCE | clu z,r0 |

### Two-Byte Instructions (Register + Immediate)

| First Byte | Instruction Format |
|------------|-------------------|
| 0x09 | add r0,imm8 |
| 0x0B | add r2,imm8 |
| 0x0C | add sp,imm8 |
| 0x13 | bra offset8 |
| 0x14 | brf offset8 |
| 0x15 | brt offset8 |
| 0x2C | lb r0,(r0) with offset |
| 0x2E | lb r0,(r2) with offset |
| 0x44 | lc r0,imm8 |
| 0x45 | lc r1,imm8 |
| 0x46 | lc r2,imm8 |
| 0x4D | lw r0,offset(fp) |
| 0x51 | lw r1,offset(fp) |
| 0x55 | lw r2,offset(fp) |
| 0x82 | sb r0,(r2) with offset |
| 0x84 | sb r1,(r0) with offset |
| 0xA6 | sw r0,offset(fp) |

### Four-Byte Instructions (Register + 24-bit Address)

| First Byte | Instruction Format |
|------------|-------------------|
| 0x29 | la r0,addr24 |
| 0x2A | la r1,addr24 |
| 0x2B | la r2,addr24 |

## Calling Convention

### Function Prologue
```asm
push    fp          ; Save frame pointer
push    r2          ; Save callee-saved register
push    r1          ; Save return address
mov     fp,sp       ; Set up frame pointer
add     sp,-N       ; Allocate stack space
```

### Function Epilogue
```asm
mov     sp,fp       ; Restore stack pointer
pop     r1          ; Restore return address
pop     r2          ; Restore callee-saved register
pop     fp          ; Restore frame pointer
jmp     (r1)        ; Return
```

### Argument Passing
- Arguments are pushed right-to-left onto the stack
- Return value is in r0
- Caller cleans up arguments with `add sp,N`

### Stack Frame Layout
```
Higher addresses
+----------------+
| Argument N     | fp + (N*3 + 6)
| ...            |
| Argument 1     | fp + 9
+----------------+
| Saved r1       | fp + 6
| Saved r2       | fp + 3
| Saved fp       | fp + 0
+----------------+
| Local var 1    | fp - 3
| Local var 2    | fp - 6
| ...            |
+----------------+
Lower addresses (sp)
```

## Word Size

- Words are 24 bits (3 bytes)
- Stack operations push/pop 3 bytes at a time
- Word addresses should be 3-byte aligned for efficiency

## Example: Fibonacci

```asm
_fib:
        push    fp              ; Save frame pointer
        push    r2              ; Save r2
        push    r1              ; Save return address
        mov     fp,sp           ; Set up frame
        add     sp,-3           ; Local variable space
        lw      r2,9(fp)        ; Load argument n

        lc      r0,2            ; Load constant 2
        cls     r2,r0           ; Compare n < 2
        brf     L17             ; Branch if false

        lc      r0,1            ; Return 1
        bra     L16             ; Jump to epilogue

L17:
        mov     r0,r2           ; r0 = n
        add     r0,-1           ; r0 = n - 1
        push    r0              ; Push argument
        la      r0,_fib         ; Load fib address
        jal     r1,(r0)         ; Call fib(n-1)
        add     sp,3            ; Clean up argument
        sw      r0,-3(fp)       ; Save result

        mov     r0,r2           ; r0 = n
        add     r0,-2           ; r0 = n - 2
        push    r0              ; Push argument
        la      r0,_fib         ; Load fib address
        jal     r1,(r0)         ; Call fib(n-2)
        add     sp,3            ; Clean up argument
        lw      r1,-3(fp)       ; Load fib(n-1)
        add     r0,r1           ; r0 = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

L16:
        mov     sp,fp           ; Restore stack
        pop     r1              ; Restore return address
        pop     r2              ; Restore r2
        pop     fp              ; Restore frame pointer
        jmp     (r1)            ; Return
```

## Interrupts

- Interrupt vector is in r6
- When an interrupt occurs, `jal r7,(r6)` is executed
- Return from interrupt with `jmp (r7)`
- Interrupts can be disabled by the `intis` flag

## References

- Verilog source: `cor24_cpu.v`
- Example programs: `demo/*.lst`
- Hardware manual: `doc/COR24-TB-MAN.pdf`
- MakerLisp: https://makerlisp.com

## License

The COR24 CPU is open-source under the MIT License.

input.description: Inspect ISA data and page list

content.subject: Write repo-local ISA reference (docs/isa.md)
content.description: Produce a standalone ISA reference in this repo: opcode/mnemonic table with binary encodings, instruction length rules, register roles (r0-r2 GP; r3=fp, r4=sp, r5=z, r6=iv, r7=ir), addressing modes, flag semantics. Copied/adapted from cor24-rs/docs/isa-reference.md and x-assembler's encode tables, with provenance notes.

content.subject: Inventory source materials in cor24-rs/docs
content.description: Read isa-reference.md, as24-notes.md, assembler-examples.md, extended-assembler.md, as24.c, ld24.c, fib.s, sieve.s, and pipeline.md. Identify which are primary (authoritative) vs. secondary (derived) and note what each covers.

content.subject: Stub FPGA runtime + tooling + oracle specs
content.description: Create placeholder docs with TODO markers for the items that aren't yet decided: docs/fpga-runtime.md (memory map, reset, UART, entry point), docs/self-host-toolchain.md (monitor/editor/linker/loader/runner contract), docs/oracle-protocol.md (REST service endpoint, request/response shape, corpus diff procedure). Each doc records what's known and explicitly lists open questions.

content.subject: Fetch ISA and hardware web pages with playwright
content.description: Try playwright-cli (or browser automation) to render the two JS SPA pages at sw-embed.github.io/web-sw-cor24-demos/#/isa and /#/hardware. Capture rendered tables and prose for cross-reference against the local docs.

content.subject: Write output format spec (docs/output-formats.md)
content.description: Document the .bin format (byte layout, no header vs. header, base address) and .lst listing format (columns, alignment, whitespace, label placement). Based on cor24-run and x-assembler's observable output on fib.s and nop.s.

content.subject: Update docs index and CLAUDE.md
content.description: Add the new docs to any index (README or CLAUDE.md), repoint prose from cor24-rs toward x-assembler as the bootstrap, and make sure the new specs are the canonical in-repo references (not sibling-repo pointers).

content.subject: Write as24 language spec (docs/as24-language.md)
content.description: Reverse-engineer from as24.c, x-assembler's parser, and the .s corpus. Cover: lexical rules (comments, whitespace, case), literal syntax (decimal/hex/char/string), label rules, mnemonic+operand grammar, directives (.org, .word, .byte, .string, etc.), expression syntax if any, forward-reference handling. Marked as normative for sw-as24 to implement.

content.subject: Repoint vendor from cor24-rs to x-assembler
content.description: Switch the bootstrap cross-assembler from cor24-rs's cor24-run to sw-cor24-x-assembler (once it has a CLI binary). Update vendor/active.env, add vendor/sw-xas24 manifest, update build.sh, update CLAUDE.md prose. Emulator usage for 'just run' becomes a separate vendored tool (sw-cor24-emulator).

content.text: Found structured Rust data modules in `src/data/isa/` that back the web pages — better than scraping. Marking task updates and pulling primary sources.

200	        mnemonic: "brt",
201	        operands: "disp",
202	        size: 2,
203	        category: InstrCategory::Branch,
204	        description: "Branch if true: if c then PC = PC+4 + disp, else fall through",
205	    },
206	    InstructionInfo {
207	        mnemonic: "brf",
208	        operands: "disp",
209	        size: 2,
210	        category: InstrCategory::Branch,
211	        description: "Branch if false: if !c then PC = PC+4 + disp, else fall through",
212	    },
213	    InstructionInfo {
214	        mnemonic: "jal",
215	        operands: "r1, (Rb)",
216	        size: 1,
217	        category: InstrCategory::CallReturn,
218	        description: "Jump and link: r1 = PC+1, PC = Rb. Target: r0, r1, or r2",
219	    },
220	    InstructionInfo {
221	        mnemonic: "jmp",
222	        operands: "(Ra)",
223	        size: 1,
224	        category: InstrCategory::CallReturn,
225	        description: "Jump register: PC = Ra. jmp (ir) also clears interrupt-in-service",
226	    },
227	    InstructionInfo {
228	        mnemonic: "push",
229	        operands: "Ra",
230	        size: 1,
231	        category: InstrCategory::Stack,
232	        description: "Push word: sp -= 3; Mem[sp] = Ra. Pushable: r0, r1, r2, fp",
233	    },
234	    InstructionInfo {
235	        mnemonic: "pop",
236	        operands: "Ra",
237	        size: 1,
238	        category: InstrCategory::Stack,
239	        description: "Pop word: Ra = Mem[sp]; sp += 3. Poppable: r0, r1, r2, fp",
240	    },
241	    InstructionInfo {
242	        mnemonic: "mov",
243	        operands: "Ra, Rb",
244	        size: 1,
245	        category: InstrCategory::Move,
246	        description: "Register copy: Ra = Rb. rb=5: Ra = c ? 1 : 0 (condition flag)",
247	    },
248	    InstructionInfo {
249	        mnemonic: "nop",
250	        operands: "-",
251	        size: 1,
252	        category: InstrCategory::Misc,
253	        description: "No operation. Byte 0xFF, advances PC by 1",
254	    },
255	    InstructionInfo {
256	        mnemonic: "halt",
257	        operands: "-",
258	        size: 1,
259	        category: InstrCategory::Misc,
260	        description: "Halt CPU. Only byte 0x00 at address 0; otherwise decodes as add r0, r0",
261	    },
262	];
263	
264	#[cfg(test)]
265	mod tests {
266	    use super::*;
267	
268	    #[test]
269	    fn total_instruction_count() {
270	        assert_eq!(all_instructions().len(), 34);
271	    }
272	
273	    #[test]
274	    fn all_instructions_have_valid_size() {
275	        for instr in all_instructions() {
276	            assert!(
277	                matches!(instr.size, 1 | 2 | 4),
278	                "invalid size {} for {}",
279	                instr.size,
280	                instr.mnemonic
281	            );
282	        }
283	    }
284	}

1	#[derive(Clone, PartialEq, Debug)]
2	pub struct RegisterInfo {

5	    pub is_gp: bool,
6	    pub can_load_dest: bool,

11	}

15	}

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""
3	Extract decode ROM values from dis_rom.v Verilog file.
4	Generates Rust code for the complete 256-entry decode table.
5	"""
6	
7	# Initialization values for each bit (0-11), from the Verilog file
8	# Format: 256-bit hex values, MSB first (bit 255 at left, bit 0 at right)
9	INITVALS = {
10	    11: "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF80000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000",
11	    10: "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF8007FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFEFEEEFFFC000000000000000",
12	    9:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFC0FFFFFFFFFFFFFF000000000100000003FFFFFFFFFF80000000",
13	    8:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF83F7FFFFFFFF80000FFFFFE000000000003FFFFFFF0007FF00000",
14	    7:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF83FFFFF000007F000FFFE01FFFD00000003F000000FC07FCFE000",
15	    6:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFE38FFC0FF80040FC0FC01E1F802FEEEC0038FFF000E207E281E00",
16	    5:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF9C0800000000400000000000003C0000000000000000000381000",
17	    4:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF824F030E078030C30C381998701B3C03C024F00F00918603E09C0",
18	    3:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFD28E0C1C0780C30C30715460E1283C03C120F00F04A619398438",
19	    2:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF83F800000000400000001FE00013E220003F000000FC000381E00",
20	    1:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFD00C90A926664A28A2A6C0054930119B333FCCCCCCFC515B95F24",
21	    0:  "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFABFA4A14955561861874A010A4B1D916AABFAAAAAAFD0C27C3E92",
22	}
23	
24	# Actually, let me re-read the Verilog file more carefully
25	# The values have 0x prefix stripped and should be exactly 64 hex chars
26	
27	import re
28	
29	def parse_verilog(filepath):
30	    """Parse dis_rom.v and extract initval patterns."""
31	    with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
32	        content = f.read()
33	
34	    # Pattern: defparam mem_0_N.initval = 256'hXXX...XXX ;
35	    pattern = r"defparam mem_0_(\d+)\.initval = 256'h([0-9A-Fa-f]+)"
36	    matches = re.findall(pattern, content)
37	
38	    initvals = {}
39	    for bit_num, hex_val in matches:
40	        initvals[int(bit_num)] = hex_val.upper()
41	        print(f"Bit {bit_num}: {len(hex_val)} hex chars")
42	
43	    return initvals
44	
45	def hex_to_bits(hex_str):
46	    """Convert hex string to bit array (bit 0 = LSB = rightmost)."""
47	    # Ensure it's 64 hex chars (256 bits)
48	    hex_str = hex_str.zfill(64)
49	
50	    # Convert to integer and extract bits
51	    value = int(hex_str, 16)
52	    bits = []
53	    for i in range(256):
54	        bits.append((value >> i) & 1)
55	    return bits
56	
57	def generate_decode_table(initvals):
58	    """Generate the 256-entry decode table."""
59	    # Convert each initval to bit array
60	    bit_arrays = {}
61	    for bit_num in range(12):
62	        bit_arrays[bit_num] = hex_to_bits(initvals[bit_num])
63	
64	    # For each address, combine bits 0-11
65	    decode_table = []
66	    for addr in range(256):
67	        value = 0
68	        for bit_num in range(12):
69	            if bit_arrays[bit_num][addr]:
70	                value |= (1 << bit_num)
71	        decode_table.append(value)
72	
73	    return decode_table
74	
75	def generate_rust_code(decode_table):
76	    """Generate Rust code for the decode ROM."""
77	    lines = []
78	    lines.append("/// Decode ROM extracted from dis_rom.v")
79	    lines.append("/// Maps 8-bit instruction byte to 12-bit decoded value: [unused:opcode(5):ra(3):rb(3)]")
80	    lines.append("pub const DECODE_ROM: [u16; 256] = [")
81	
82	    for i in range(0, 256, 8):
83	        row = decode_table[i:i+8]
84	        hex_vals = [f"0x{v:03X}" for v in row]
85	        comment = f"// 0x{i:02X}-0x{i+7:02X}"
86	        lines.append(f"    {', '.join(hex_vals)}, {comment}")
87	
88	    lines.append("];")
89	    return '\n'.join(lines)
90	
91	def analyze_opcodes(decode_table):
92	    """Analyze the decode table to understand opcode mappings."""
93	    print("\n=== Opcode Analysis ===")
94	
95	    opcode_names = {
96	        0x00: "AddReg", 0x01: "AddImm", 0x02: "And", 0x03: "Bra",
97	        0x04: "Brf", 0x05: "Brt", 0x06: "Ceq", 0x07: "Cls",
98	        0x08: "Clu", 0x09: "Jal", 0x0A: "Jmp", 0x0B: "La",
99	        0x0C: "Lb", 0x0D: "Lbu", 0x0E: "Lc", 0x0F: "Lcu",
100	        0x10: "Lw", 0x11: "Mov", 0x12: "Mul", 0x13: "Or",
101	        0x14: "Pop", 0x15: "Push", 0x16: "Sb", 0x17: "Shl",
102	        0x18: "Sra", 0x19: "Srl", 0x1A: "Sub", 0x1B: "SubSp",
103	        0x1C: "Sw", 0x1D: "Sxt", 0x1E: "Xor", 0x1F: "Zxt",
104	    }
105	
106	    reg_names = ["r0", "r1", "r2", "fp", "sp", "z", "r6", "r7"]
107	
108	    valid_count = 0
109	    invalid_count = 0
110	
111	    for addr in range(256):
112	        decoded = decode_table[addr]
113	        if decoded == 0xFFF:
114	            invalid_count += 1
115	            continue
116	
117	        valid_count += 1
118	        opcode = (decoded >> 6) & 0x1F
119	        ra = (decoded >> 3) & 0x07
120	        rb = decoded & 0x07
121	
122	        op_name = opcode_names.get(opcode, f"???({opcode:02X})")
123	        print(f"0x{addr:02X} -> opcode={op_name:8s} ra={reg_names[ra]:3s} rb={reg_names[rb]:3s}  (raw: 0x{decoded:03X})")
124	
125	    print(f"\nValid entries: {valid_count}")
126	    print(f"Invalid entries (0xFFF): {invalid_count}")
127	
128	def generate_encoding_tables(decode_table):
129	    """Generate reverse mapping: (opcode, ra, rb) -> byte."""
130	    # Group by opcode
131	    opcode_encodings = {}
132	
133	    for byte_val in range(256):
134	        decoded = decode_table[byte_val]
135	        if decoded == 0xFFF:
136	            continue
137	
138	        opcode = (decoded >> 6) & 0x1F
139	        ra = (decoded >> 3) & 0x07
140	        rb = decoded & 0x07
141	
142	        if opcode not in opcode_encodings:
143	            opcode_encodings[opcode] = {}
144	
145	        # Key is (ra, rb) tuple encoded as ra*8+rb
146	        key = ra * 8 + rb
147	        opcode_encodings[opcode][key] = byte_val
148	
149	    return opcode_encodings
150	
151	def generate_encoding_rust_code(decode_table):
152	    """Generate Rust code for instruction encoding."""
153	    encodings = generate_encoding_tables(decode_table)
154	
155	    opcode_names = {
156	        0x00: "AddReg", 0x01: "AddImm", 0x02: "And", 0x03: "Bra",
157	        0x04: "Brf", 0x05: "Brt", 0x06: "Ceq", 0x07: "Cls",
158	        0x08: "Clu", 0x09: "Jal", 0x0A: "Jmp", 0x0B: "La",
159	        0x0C: "Lb", 0x0D: "Lbu", 0x0E: "Lc", 0x0F: "Lcu",
160	        0x10: "Lw", 0x11: "Mov", 0x12: "Mul", 0x13: "Or",
161	        0x14: "Pop", 0x15: "Push", 0x16: "Sb", 0x17: "Shl",
162	        0x18: "Sra", 0x19: "Srl", 0x1A: "Sub", 0x1B: "SubSp",
163	        0x1C: "Sw", 0x1D: "Sxt", 0x1E: "Xor", 0x1F: "Zxt",
164	    }
165	
166	    reg_names = ["r0", "r1", "r2", "fp", "sp", "z", "r6", "r7"]
167	
168	    lines = []
169	    lines.append("//! COR24 instruction encoding tables")
170	    lines.append("//! Auto-generated from dis_rom.v - do not edit manually")
171	    lines.append("//!")
172	    lines.append("//! Generated by: scripts/extract_decode_rom.py")
173	    lines.append("//! Provides reverse mapping: (opcode, ra, rb) -> instruction byte")
174	    lines.append("")
175	    lines.append("use crate::cpu::instruction::Opcode;")
176	    lines.append("")
177	
178	    # Generate a single large encoding function
179	    lines.append("/// Encode instruction to byte. Returns None if encoding not found.")
180	    lines.append("/// For single-byte instructions, returns the opcode byte.")
181	    lines.append("/// For multi-byte instructions, returns the first byte (opcode + register info).")
182	    lines.append("pub fn encode_instruction(opcode: Opcode, ra: u8, rb: u8) -> Option<u8> {")
183	    lines.append("    let ra = ra & 0x07;")
184	    lines.append("    let rb = rb & 0x07;")
185	    lines.append("    ")
186	    lines.append("    match opcode {")
187	
188	    for opcode in sorted(encodings.keys()):
189	        op_name = opcode_names.get(opcode, f"Unknown{opcode:02X}")
190	        enc_map = encodings[opcode]
191	
192	        lines.append(f"        Opcode::{op_name} => match (ra, rb) {{")
193	
194	        for key in sorted(enc_map.keys()):
195	            ra = key // 8
196	            rb = key % 8
197	            byte_val = enc_map[key]
198	            lines.append(f"            ({ra}, {rb}) => Some(0x{byte_val:02X}), // {reg_names[ra]},{reg_names[rb]}")
199	
200	        lines.append("            _ => None,")
201	        lines.append("        },")
202	
203	    lines.append("        Opcode::Invalid => None,")
204	    lines.append("    }")
205	    lines.append("}")
206	    lines.append("")
207	
208	    # Generate convenience functions for common patterns
209	    lines.append("/// Encode add register instruction: add ra,rb")
210	    lines.append("pub fn encode_add_reg(ra: u8, rb: u8) -> Option<u8> {")
211	    lines.append("    encode_instruction(Opcode::AddReg, ra, rb)")
212	    lines.append("}")
213	    lines.append("")
214	
215	    lines.append("/// Encode add immediate instruction first byte: add ra,imm8")
